abstract:
Aqueous zinc metal batteries (ZIBs) are significantly limited by parasitic water corrosion, as solvated zinc ions continuously transport active water molecules to the electrode/electrolyte interface. In this work, we develop a sacrificial solvation shell strategy using a methanol-based electrolyte additive to displace water from the Zn²⁺ coordination sphere and facilitate the in situ formation of a protective interphase. The results demonstrate that the addition of methanol reconfigures the solvation structure to Zn(H₂O)₂(CH₃OH)₂²⁺. The simultaneous decomposition of methanol and Zn(CF₃SO₃)₂ salts leads to a fluoride-rich, organic–inorganic gradient solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) characterized by an organic-rich surface and an inorganic ZnF₂-rich foundation. This unique gradient structure marries the advantages of rapid ionic conductivity with high mechanical flexibility.

Consequently, the methanol-modified electrolyte (Me56) enables highly reversible, corrosion-free zinc plating and stripping on copper foils for 1,000 cycles with a remarkable average Coulombic efficiency of 99.95%. In symmetric Zn//Zn cells, the electrolyte supports a record-breaking cumulative plating capacity of 10 Ah/cm² at a high current density of 40 mA/cm². Furthermore, practical VO₂//Zn full cells utilizing a 20-μm-thick zinc foil at an ultra-low N/P ratio of 2 and a high areal capacity of 4.7 mAh/cm² exhibit stable operation for over 250 cycles. These findings suggest that the use of a sacrificial solvation shell to engineer gradient interphases provides a robust pathway for the development of commercial-scale, high-capacity zinc batteries for stationary energy storage.
